does: This paper presents ZeroUnlearn, a few-shot machine unlearning framework that removes sensitive information from large language models through model editing rather than retraining. The method reformulates unlearning as a knowledge re-mapping problem, where sensitive inputs are mapped to neutral target states while their original representations are removed via representational orthogonality constraints. The approach uses multiplicative parameter updates with a closed-form solution to achieve efficient, targeted unlearning of individual or multiple samples. Experiments show ZeroUnlearn outperforms existing baselines while maintaining the model's general performance and knowledge of related concepts, addressing the computational expense and utility degradation problems of previous retraining-based methods.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of machine unlearning and model editing literature. It extends prior approaches that attempted unlearning through expensive retraining or aggressive fine-tuning by adopting the model editing paradigm, which has recently shown promise for surgical knowledge modification in neural networks. The paper connects to research on representational geometry and orthogonality constraints in neural networks, as well as prior work on few-shot knowledge manipulation. It responds to privacy and safety concerns raised by the massive memorization in large language models trained on web-scale corpora, aligning with growing work on identifying and mitigating harmful retained information without sacrificing overall model capability.
enables: This framework opens directions for scalable privacy-preserving deployment of large language models, enabling practical solutions to remove memorized sensitive data on-demand without full model retraining. The closed-form solution and few-shot nature suggest potential for efficient batch unlearning of multiple sensitive inputs, supporting regulatory compliance and rapid response to privacy violations. The orthogonality-based approach may generalize to other model editing objectives and multi-modal systems. Future work could combine ZeroUnlearn with methods for detecting sensitive information, enable personalized unlearning across different users, and extend the technique to larger model families and other architectures beyond transformers.
